subject: ap-chemistry
title: Solubility & Common Ion Effect
stimulus: A student is studying the properties of $\text{CaSO}_4$ and $\text{PbSO}_4$. The student has samples of both compounds, which are white powders.

parts.prompt: The student tests the electrical conductivity of each solid and observes that neither solid conducts electricity. Describe the structures of the solids that account for their inability to conduct electricity.
parts.scoring_notes: Both $\text{CaSO}_4$ and $\text{PbSO}_4$ are ionic solids. In the solid state, the ions are held in fixed lattice positions and cannot migrate. Electrical conduction requires mobile charge carriers (free ions or electrons). Because the ions are immobile in the crystal lattice, no current flows.

scoring_points.description: For a correct description citing immobile ions in the solid-state lattice: 1 point

required_elements: Ionic solids have ions held in fixed lattice positions; they cannot move to carry charge.
alternatives.correct_example: Both $\text{CaSO}_4$ and $\text{PbSO}_4$ are ionic compounds. In the solid state, the cations ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$ or $\text{Pb}^{2+}$) and anions ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) are held in a rigid crystal lattice and cannot move freely. Because electrical conduction requires mobile ions or electrons, neither solid conducts electricity.

wrong_examples: Saying the solids are covalent — both are ionic compounds with metal cations and polyatomic anions.
Saying there are no charged particles — ionic solids do contain charged ions, but they are fixed in place.
common_traps: Ionic solids conduct electricity only when melted or dissolved in water (ions become mobile). In the solid state, the lattice holds ions in fixed positions → no electrical conduction.

parts.prompt: Which compound is more soluble in water, $\text{CaSO}_4$(s) or $\text{PbSO}_4$(s)? Justify your answer based on the results of the conductivity test.
parts.scoring_notes: $\text{CaSO}_4$ is more soluble. When dissolved, these ionic solids dissociate into free ions that carry current. Higher conductivity of the $\text{CaSO}_4$ solution indicates a higher ion concentration, which means more $\text{CaSO}_4$ dissolved — i.e., greater solubility.

scoring_points.description: For identifying $\text{CaSO}_4$ as more soluble and citing higher conductivity as evidence: 1 point

required_elements: Higher electrical conductivity of the $\text{CaSO}_4$ solution indicates more dissolved ions, meaning greater solubility.
alternatives.correct_example: $\text{CaSO}_4$ is more soluble. The $\text{CaSO}_4$ solution has higher electrical conductivity than the $\text{PbSO}_4$ solution, indicating a higher concentration of ions ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) in solution. Because more $\text{CaSO}_4$ has dissolved to produce these ions, $\text{CaSO}_4$ is more soluble.

wrong_examples: Saying $\text{PbSO}_4$ is more soluble — the data show the $\text{CaSO}_4$ solution has higher conductivity.
Citing color or appearance rather than conductivity — the experimental evidence given is conductivity.
common_traps: Higher conductivity = more dissolved ions = greater extent of dissolution = higher solubility. This is the direct experimental evidence linking conductivity to solubility.

parts.prompt: Draw a particulate representation of $\text{PbSO}_4$(s) and the ions dissolved in the solution in the beaker on the right in the diagram. Draw the particles to look like those shown to the right of the beaker. Draw an appropriate number of dissolved ions relative to the number of dissolved ions in the beaker on the left.
parts.scoring_notes: The drawing should show solid $\text{PbSO}_4$ at the bottom of the beaker (excess undissolved solid) and fewer $\text{Pb}^{2+}$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions in solution compared to the $\text{CaSO}_4$ beaker. Equal numbers of cations and anions must be shown (1:1 ratio from $\text{PbSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Pb}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$). The number of dissolved ions must be visibly fewer than the left beaker.

parts.prompt: The student attempts to increase the solubility of $\text{CaSO}_4$(s) by adding 10.0 mL of 2 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$(aq) to the beaker, and observes that additional precipitate forms in the beaker. Explain this observation.
parts.scoring_notes: Adding $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ increases $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]$ in solution (common ion effect). According to Le Chatelier's principle, increasing $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]$ shifts the dissolution equilibrium $\text{CaSO}_4(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq)$ to the left, causing more $\text{CaSO}_4$ to precipitate. The ion product $Q = [\text{Ca}^{2+}][\text{SO}_4^{2-}]$ exceeds $K_{sp}$, driving precipitation.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing increased $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]$ and Le Chatelier's principle (common ion effect): 1 point

required_elements: The equilibrium shifts left (toward precipitation of $\text{CaSO}_4$).
alternatives.correct_example: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is a strong acid and fully dissociates, contributing $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions to the solution. This increases $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]$, making $Q = [\text{Ca}^{2+}][\text{SO}_4^{2-}] > K_{sp}(\text{CaSO}_4)$. By Le Chatelier's principle, the system responds by shifting the equilibrium to the left ($\text{CaSO}_4$ precipitates) until $Q$ returns to $K_{sp}$, reducing solubility.

wrong_examples: Saying the reaction becomes more acidic so the solid forms — acidity alone is not the mechanism here; the $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ common ion is responsible.
Saying solubility increases — the observation is that a precipitate forms, indicating decreased solubility.
common_traps: Common ion effect: adding a soluble source of an ion already present in the equilibrium suppresses dissolution (shifts equilibrium toward the solid). Here $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ is the common ion; adding $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ raises $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]$, causing $\text{CaSO}_4$ to precipitate.
